headline: Missoula\'s Troop D Mustered for Spanish-American War, 1898
body:
The air in Missoula, on that thirteenth day of May, 1898, was thick with a peculiar tension, a blend of raw patriotism and the unspoken dread of the unknown. War had been declared, a distant rumble against Spain, yet its echo reached even the farthest valleys of Montana. Will Cave, a man of swift action and keen eye, had, in the immediate aftermath of the Maine’s demise, rallied fifty souls to the banner in mere hours. Now, the full complement of eighty-three men, drawn from the rugged heart of Missoula and its surrounding territories, stood ready. They were no longer merely Troop D; they were the 3rd U.S. Volunteer Cavalry, Troop F, a designation that carried the weight of national expectation and the promise of distant battlefields.

Captain Frank Higgins, a figure of stern resolve, and First Lieutenant Will Cave, his equally determined second, stood at their head. These were men forged in the hard school of the West, accustomed to the bite of winter wind and the unforgiving vastness of the land. Their command, a motley collection of cowboys, miners, and homesteaders, gathered at Fort Missoula. Here, the raw material of civilian life was to be hammered into the disciplined form of soldiers. The days were filled with the clang of equipment, the barked commands of drill sergeants, and the sweat of men pushing their bodies to the limits. They learned the rhythm of cavalry life, the feel of a rifle in their hands, the grim purpose of their undertaking.

Then came the word, a telegram from the distant east, on May 22: Camp Thomas, Chicamauga, Tennessee. The destination was set. Missoula, a town accustomed to the quiet rhythms of frontier life, erupted in a spontaneous outpouring of support. Hasty decorations adorned the streets, and a parade was hastily arranged for Higgins Avenue. The university band struck up martial tunes, veterans marched with a renewed sense of purpose, the mayor offered solemn words, and schoolchildren waved small flags. Three thousand strong, the crowd pressed against the railroad depot, a human tide of farewells. The reporter for the Daily Democrat-Messenger captured the raw emotion of the moment, writing of “fond farewells and tearful adieus, some of which tried even the strongest heart ... the wet eyes of the many relatives who were looking at their sons and brothers for probably the last time.” It was a scene etched in the collective memory, a testament to the profound human cost of war, even before the first shot was fired. As the train pulled away, the band played “The Girl I Left Behind Me,” a poignant counterpoint to the cheers and sobs. A small, shaggy dog, Private Story’s mascot, “Admiral Dewey,” barked from an open window, a symbol of the innocence and hope that still clung to the departing men.

The journey was long, a blur of changing landscapes and the constant rumble of the train. The men, these
